symbols: triangular pediment — classical temple form adopted for Christian worship
arched entrance — gateway to sacred space
pilasters — structural order reflecting divine order
description: A photograph of the facade of the Basilica of Sant'Andrea in Mantua, designed by Leon Battista Alberti. The Renaissance church front features a monumental triumphal arch motif with a large central arched entrance flanked by smaller bays with pilasters. A classical triangular pediment crowns the facade. Behind and above rises a large dome. A bell tower from an older structure is visible to the left. The facade is rendered in warm stone tones with white marble elements.
theological_description: The Basilica of Sant'Andrea in Mantua, designed by the great Renaissance architect Leon Battista Alberti (begun 1472), represents the synthesis of classical Roman architecture with Christian sacred space. The triumphal arch motif of the facade deliberately evokes Roman triumphal arches, reimagined as a gateway to sacred space — Christ's triumph over death made manifest in architectural form. The church houses the relic of the Holy Blood, believed to be earth soaked with the blood of Christ brought by the Roman centurion Longinus. The architectural grandeur reflects the belief that the house of God should mirror heavenly glory (Exodus 25:8-9).

scripture_refs.reason: Making a sanctuary so God may dwell among His people, the theology behind church building
I was glad when they said to me, let us go to the house of the Lord
The dwelling of God with humanity, anticipated in sacred architecture
